chunks.text: Joseph Thomas Gier was appointed associate professor of electrical engineering at UC Berkeley in 1952, becoming the first tenured Black professor in the entire University of California educational system and the first tenured Black faculty member in a STEM field at a top-ranked, predominantly white university in the country. Many celebrate statistician David Blackwell as the first Black ladder-rank professor at Berkeley, but Gier was acknowledged as the first until at least 2003. It would be correct to say that David Blackwell was the first Black full professor at UC Berkeley, while Gier became a full professor in 1958.

Joe Gier was born in New Orleans on July 2, 1910, and moved to Oakland as a child. He attended Roosevelt High School, where he was an honor roll student involved in various activities. After high school, he worked briefly before being accepted to UC Berkeley in 1930. He earned his Bachelor of Science in Mechanical Engineering in 1933, becoming the earliest known Black student to earn a B.S. in ME from Berkeley. After graduation, he worked as a draftsman before returning to Berkeley in 1937, earning his Master's degree in Engineering in 1940.

Gier formed a professional partnership with ME Prof. Robert Valentine Dunkle in 1943. They helped define the use of spectral selectivity for efficient photothermal conversion of solar radiation and became world experts in thermal and luminous radiation. Gier was listed on eight patents filed between 1948 and 1960, with six listing him as the primary contributor. Their inventions included the Gier Dunkle Total Hemispherical Radiometer, Infrared Reflectometer, and Black Body Reflectometer, which were used in space laboratories and solar energy research.

Gier began his career at UC Berkeley in 1939 as a lab technician for the California Highway Patrol Illumination Laboratory. He was promoted to Chief Engineer in 1941 and hired as a half-time lecturer in the EE Division in 1946. He was considered an excellent laboratory instructor and supervised the Thermal Radiation Project for the Office of Naval Research. He was an early proponent of solar energy research, presenting at the first international Conference on Solar Energy in 1955. He was elected to Sigma Xi in 1949 and promoted to Full Professor in 1958.

Gier was known for his patience, kindness, and compassion, taking a deep personal interest in his students. He received awards from the Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity and the Los Angeles Urban League for his community service. In 1958, he transferred to UCLA due to his growing interest in solar energy and health issues. He died on June 22, 1961, at age 50. The Morrin-Gier-Martinelli Heat Transfer Memorial Laboratory at UCLA was partially named in his honor. His legacy includes inspiring many and setting a precedent for future academics.

Gier's legacy was forgotten due to his untimely death in 1961 at age 50 and his transfer to UCLA three years prior. Recognition of African American contributions became more prominent after the Black Power movement, which peaked after his death. Additionally, solar energy was still considered primitive in the 1960s and did not gain widespread attention until the 1970s energy crisis. Although his achievements were noted in The Journal of Blacks in Higher Education, society was not fully prepared to recognize them at the time.

Joseph and Kathryn Gier's son, Keith, graduated from Cal Poly with a BS in Mechanical Engineering in 1971 and attended graduate school at UCLA, specializing in heat transfer. He was advised by one of his father's former students and worked as an aerospace engineer at TRW Defense and Space Systems Group, with a mentor who was also a former student of his father. After retiring from Boeing in 2014, Keith has continued working as a contractor at places like NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory and Lockheed Martin, following in his father's footsteps.
